title: About
text:
This project combines historical and real-time financial data with AI to produce automated research reports for US growth stock investors. By feeding macroeconomic indicators, equity market data, and commodity prices into an AI model, each report identifies historical patterns, draws parallels to current conditions, and highlights risks and opportunities for growth-oriented portfolios.
All charts and visualizations in the reports are created by the author — the AI generates only the written analysis.

title: About
text:
Data Sources
Every report is grounded in real data from:

FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data): Inflation (CPI), GDP growth, unemployment, Fed balance sheet, Treasury yields, household debt, recession indicators, Fed funds rate, and high-yield credit spreads
Yahoo Finance: MSCI World Index, S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, Dow Jones, VIX, gold, and Brent crude oil
Multpl.com: S&P 500 trailing P/E ratios

title: About
text:
Disclaimer
This site is for informational and educational purposes only. Nothing here constitutes financial advice. The AI-generated analysis may contain errors, hallucinations, or outdated information. Always do your own research and consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.

title: Market Research
text:
The Great Rotation: Navigating Stagflation Signals and Valuation Extremes in March 2026
Global financial markets have endured a turbulent first quarter, driven by geopolitical shocks and a sharp reassessment of risk assets. The escalation of conflict in the Middle East has pushed Brent crude to $100 a barrel, wiping an estimated $7 trillion off global equity valuations as investors flee energy-intensive tech giants reuters.com. We are witnessing a distinct rotation away from US large-cap growth toward value, small caps, and ex-US equities, with the MSCI ACWI ex-USA posting a 4.4% year-to-date gain compared to the Nasdaq-100’s -0.6% decline nasdaq.com. Volatility has surged alongside sovereign yields, with the VIX climbing significantly and 10-year Treasury yields moving sideways amidst concerns over stagflation bis.org. While some strategists anticipate AI-driven productivity gains may eventually dampen inflation, the immediate outlook is defined by supply shocks and fragile risk sentiment gs.com.
Turning to the US domestic landscape, the data paints a picture of a late-cycle economy grappling with Sticky inflation and slowing momentum. As of February 2026, CPI inflation sits at 2.4%, a moderate figure that masks underlying pressure from energy costs, while GDP growth decelerated to just 0.7% in Q4 2025. This combination of sub-1% growth and rising commodity prices echoes the stagflationary undertones of the 1970s, though unemployment remains contained at 4.4% as of February 2026, preventing a full-blown recessionary signal for now. The Federal Reserve’s balance sheet has contracted to approximately $6.65 trillion as of March 2026, removing a significant liquidity cushion that previously supported high-multiple assets.



US Inflation Rate (YoY)





US GDP Growth Rate (YoY)


Valuation metrics suggest we are testing the limits of investor patience. The S&P 500 trailing P/E ratio reached 27.25 in March 2026, levels not seen since the peak of the dot-com bubble and the 2021 liquidity boom. While earnings have grown, the price paid for that growth is historically expensive, particularly when juxtaposed against a 10-year Treasury yield of 4.24% as of March 2026. In the 2000 cycle, similar valuation extremes preceded a decade of mean reversion; today, the presence of tangible AI revenue provides more fundamental support than the speculative internet plays of the past, but the mathematical gravity of high rates on long-duration cash flows remains a headwind. The Nasdaq 100 closed at 22,953.38 in March 2026, showing resilience but facing stiff resistance as yields climb.



S&P 500 PE Ratio





Performance of Major US Stock Indices


Market stress is becoming visible in volatility and credit spreads. The VIX spiked to 30.61 in March 2026, a level associated with significant market dislocations like the 2008 financial crisis or the 2020 pandemic onset, indicating that hedging costs are elevated. Despite this fear, the high-yield option-adjusted spread remains relatively tight at 3.42 as of March 2026, suggesting credit markets have not yet priced in a systemic Default risk. This divergence between equity volatility and credit stability is characteristic of late-cycle environments where equity investors price in growth slowdowns before bond investors acknowledge default risks. The yield curve has steepened, with the 2-year yield at 3.71% and the 10-year at 4.24%, removing the inversion that previously signaled recession, but the absolute level of rates continues to pressure leveraged balance sheets.



Cboe Volatility Index (VIX)





US 2Y and 10Y Treasury Yields and Spread


Commodity markets are flashing warning signals that resemble the 1970s oil shocks. Brent crude oil hit $108.79 in March 2026, while gold surged to $4,540.40, reflecting a flight to safety and currency debasement fears. This commodity strength is inflationary and acts as a tax on consumer discretionary spending, directly impacting the top-line growth of many US technology and consumer companies. Historically, sustained oil prices above $100 without corresponding productivity gains have led to margin compression across the S&P 500. However, the household debt service ratio remains manageable at 11.32% as of Q4 2025, providing a buffer against immediate consumption collapse.



Gold Prices





Brent Oil Prices


Based on these patterns, two distinct risks and opportunities emerge for growth investors. The valuation compression risk is paramount; with P/E ratios at 27.25 and 10-year yields above 4%, there is limited room for multiple expansion, meaning stock performance will rely entirely on earnings growth which is threatened by the 0.7% GDP backdrop. Additionally, the stagflation risk posed by $108 oil and slowing GDP could force the Fed to maintain higher rates for longer, further discounting long-duration cash flows. Conversely, the AI productivity opportunity remains intact; if AI implementation drives marginal cost reductions as strategists hope, it could offset wage inflation and protect margins for adopters. Finally, the volatility spike creates an opportunity to acquire high-quality compounders at discounts; companies with strong free cash flow and low debt are historically resilient during VIX spikes above 30.

Risk: Valuation Compression. With the S&P 500 P/E at 27.25 and the 10-year yield at 4.24%, the equity risk premium is thin. Any disappointment in earnings growth due to the 0.7% GDP slowdown could trigger a sharp de-rating.
Risk: Stagflationary Pressure. Brent oil at $108.79 combined with 0.7% GDP growth mirrors 1970s supply shocks, threatening to erode consumer purchasing power and input margins for growth companies.
Opportunity: Quality Compounders. The VIX at 30.61 has punished speculative names disproportionately. This allows for accumulation of profitable growth stocks with strong balance sheets at more reasonable entry multiples.
Opportunity: AI-Driven Efficiency. Companies leveraging AI to reduce operating costs may outperform peers, as productivity gains can act as a disinflationary force even in a high-commodity price environment.

Given the elevated volatility, rich valuations, and stagflationary signals, I recommend a 50-75% portfolio exposure to US growth stocks. This range acknowledges the long-term innovation cycle, particularly in AI, which supports higher equilibrium valuations, but it respects the immediate headwinds of 4%+ yields and $100+ oil. Investors should prioritize companies with positive free cash flow and pricing power to navigate the potential earnings volatility ahead, avoiding speculative pre-profit names until the VIX subsides below 20.



MSCI World Equity Index





Federal Funds Target Rate (Upper Limit)
